metadata.summary: C# .NET Core backend umbrella: ASP.NET Core APIs, EF Core ORM, xUnit testing, BackgroundService. Covers layering, DI, async/await, and testing patterns.

content.title: C# .NET Core Backend Development
content.summary: Greenfield ASP.NET Core 8/9 services integrate layered controller/service/repository architecture, Entity Framework Core for data access, xUnit for unit and integration testing, and BackgroundService for async work. All async methods accept CancellationToken. Configure DI to register DbContext as scoped, hosted services as singleton, and resolve scoped dependencies inside background services via IServiceProvider.CreateScope(). Use AutoMapper to project entities to DTOs. Return ProblemDetails for errors. Organize by feature folders (Features/Users/) not technical layers.

why.p: ASP.NET Core's dependency injection and middleware pipeline make it natural to structure applications with layered boundaries: Controllers → Services → Repositories → DbContext. This prevents tight coupling and makes unit testing tractable. Entity Framework Core is a mature ORM that abstracts database details; combined with migrations, it keeps schema and code in sync. xUnit is the standard testing framework.

BackgroundService + Channel<T> enable in-process async work without deploying a separate Worker Service. IOptions pattern for configuration, ILogger for structured logging, and OpenTelemetry instrumentation are built into the framework.

This methodology is an umbrella aggregating sibling methodologies (csharp-aspnet-core, csharp-entity-framework, csharp-xunit-testing, csharp-background-services). Edits should happen in those subdirs for module coherence.

list.item: Greenfield ASP.NET Core 8/9 service: REST/gRPC API, EF Core data layer, xUnit tests, BackgroundService for async work.
Brownfield .NET Framework → .NET 8 migration where the agent needs a single map of patterns covering controllers, EF, tests, and hosted services.
Internal enterprise APIs where DI, options pattern, and configuration binding are part of the contract.
Tiny CLI utilities — dotnet new console is enough; agents should not impose Controller/Service/Repository on a 200-line script.
Highly dynamic plugin systems — F# or scripting is a better fit; .NET reflection-heavy plugin loading trips up codegen.
Functional/event-sourced cores — use F# or a CQRS framework (MediatR + Marten/EventStore) directly; this umbrella's Repository pattern fights that grain.

section.name: aspnet-core-controllers
section.p: Controllers route HTTP requests, validate input, call services, and return responses. Every action MUST be async and accept CancellationToken. Use [Authorize] and [ProducesResponseType] on every action. Never inject DbContext directly; always route through a service interface.

rule.statement: Every async controller action MUST accept CancellationToken as the last parameter and thread it to the service layer.
rule.rationale: Without token plumbing, client disconnects and timeouts do not cancel database queries, causing thread-pool starvation under load.

example.title: REST controller with service injection

section.name: services-and-di
section.p: Services implement business logic and orchestrate repositories. Always expose a public interface; the controller depends on the interface, not the concrete implementation. Register in Program.cs with appropriate lifetimes: DbContext as Scoped, IHttpClientFactory-backed clients as Transient, hosted services as Singleton. DI errors surface at startup, not at request time, because of Generic Host validation.

example.title: Service interface and implementation

section.name: entity-framework-core
section.p: Entity Framework Core maps domain entities to database tables via DbContext. Configure entities with IEntityTypeConfiguration<T> for fluent API clarity. Use migrations to version schema. Always use AsNoTracking() on read paths and ExecuteUpdateAsync/ExecuteDeleteAsync for bulk operations. Do not return IQueryable<T> from repositories; materialize with ToListAsync/ToArrayAsync inside the repo.

example.title: Entity and DbContext configuration
Repository pattern with AsNoTracking and bulk operations

section.name: xunit-testing
section.p: Write unit tests with Moq for dependencies and integration tests with WebApplicationFactory for end-to-end flows. One assertion concern per test. Use IClassFixture for shared setup and Testcontainers for real databases. Every test MUST verify a single behavior.

example.title: Unit test with Moq
Integration test with WebApplicationFactory

section.name: background-services
section.p: Use BackgroundService + Channel<T> for in-process async work. Always bound the channel with explicit capacity. Resolve scoped dependencies (DbContext, services) inside ExecuteAsync via IServiceProvider.CreateScope(). Register via AddHostedService<T>() in Program.cs; forgetting this is a common silent bug.

example.title: Queue consumer background service
Program.cs registration for background services

list.item: All public methods that touch IO must be async and accept CancellationToken. Agents drop the token frequently — review must enforce.
Register DbContext as Scoped, IHttpClientFactory-backed clients as Transient, hosted services as Singleton and resolve scoped deps via IServiceProvider.CreateScope().
Use IOptions<T> pattern for configuration; never read IConfiguration deep in services.
For EF Core: AsNoTracking() on read paths, AsSplitQuery() when projecting collections, ExecuteUpdateAsync/ExecuteDeleteAsync for bulk ops.
xUnit: one assertion concern per test; use IClassFixture for shared, ICollectionFixture for cross-class shared (sparingly).
Use record for DTOs (immutable, value equality); class for entities (EF needs setters).
Centralize DateTime.UtcNow behind TimeProvider (.NET 8) so tests can fake clock.
Add health checks for every external dependency: AddHealthChecks().AddNpgSql(...).AddRedis(...).
Use ProblemDetails (RFC 7807) for error responses; configure AddProblemDetails() in Program.cs.

antipattern.description: async void for event handlers — silent crashes.
antipattern.reason: One unhandled exception inside async void crashes the host. Force async Task.
Deadlock under sync context; agent code review must reject any sync-over-async.
Verify boot; MapperConfiguration.AssertConfigurationIsValid() in a unit test.

antipattern.description: EF queries missing await on ToListAsync().
DbContext or other scoped service injected into BackgroundService constructor.
antipattern.reason: BackgroundService is singleton; scoped services throw at runtime on first use. Mandate IServiceScopeFactory pattern.

antipattern.description: Migrations against shared dev/prod DB without human checkpoint.
antipattern.reason: Destructive — always pause before dotnet ef database update on shared DBs.

antipattern.description: Source-generated mappers (Mapperly) missing partial class keyword.
antipattern.reason: Agent often forgets the partial keyword.
